explicit_text: My parents were both born and raised in Laos before they had to leave because communism was spreading rapidly and causing displacement. My mother lived there until she was about five years old, and my father spent his childhood there as well. Laos was the place they had to give up in order to seek a better life elsewhere due to the political turmoil and government corruption.
implicit_text: My parents were both born and raised in a Southeast Asian country before they had to leave because communism was spreading rapidly and causing displacement. My mother lived there until she was about five years old, and my father spent his childhood there as well. That country was the place they had to give up in order to seek a better life elsewhere due to the political turmoil and government corruption.

explicit_text: After leaving Laos, both sides of my family ended up in Thailand where they lived in refugee camps and with relatives. My mother moved there to live with her aunt and uncle and helped work in the rice fields while attending school and learning Thai. Thailand was also where my parents participated in cultural events like Lao and Thai New Year and where they prepared for their eventual move to the United States.

explicit_text: Both my parents immigrated to the United States after spending time in refugee camps in Thailand; my dad arrived in 1980 and my mom in 1989. They came here with very little but were sponsored by family and others who helped them get started. The U.S. became the place where they built new lives, worked hard, and preserved our Lao culture despite the challenges of starting over.

explicit_text: Celebrating Lao New Year has always been an important tradition in my family, even after moving to the United States. My mother participated in it back in Thailand, dancing the traditional Fon dance, and we continue to celebrate it every April with food, songs, and dancing. It’s a joyful event that connects me to my heritage and keeps our culture alive.

explicit_text: Fon is the traditional Lao and Thai dance that my mother used to perform during cultural events like Lao and Thai New Year when she was living in Thailand. It’s a beautiful dance that represents our heritage, and it was part of how she stayed connected to our culture even while living away from Laos. This dance is a symbol of our traditions that my family continues to honor.

explicit_text: When my mother’s side of the family first arrived in the United States in 1989, they settled in Stockton, California. They were hosted by one of her aunts who lived there, which helped them get started in a new country. Stockton was the first place where my mom’s family began their journey toward building a new life in America.

explicit_text: My father’s side of the family arrived in Elgin, Illinois in 1980, where they were hosted by a white family that helped many Laotian and Southeast Asian immigrants. My dad worked at a fast food chain while going to school, and his grandparents worked in a factory. Elgin was where my dad’s family first established themselves in the U.S. and began adapting to their new life.

explicit_text: North Carolina holds a special place in my family’s story because it’s where my mom and dad reunited at a Lao Buddhist temple after a period of being apart. This reunion was a turning point that allowed them to continue their relationship and eventually start a family. The Lao Buddhist temple in North Carolina was more than just a religious place; it was where our family’s future began.

explicit_text: The Lao Buddhist temple has been a central part of my family’s cultural and spiritual life in the United States. It’s where my parents reunited in North Carolina and where we go to receive blessings from monks and honor our ancestors by offering food. The temple helps us stay connected to our Lao heritage and maintain our traditions even far from Laos.
